skill.name: clinical-reports
skill.description: Draft and validate clinical reports, including CARE case reports, diagnostic reports, clinical trial documents, SOAP notes, history and physicals, and discharge summaries.
skill.summary: Draft clinical reports with templates, terminology checks, and compliance review support.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk alerts, but review found the weak-crypto and Ruby backtick findings are mostly false positives from medical abbreviations, markdown code fences, and clinical examples. The remaining real risk is legitimate local processing of sensitive clinical files with optional user-selected output paths and Bash-enabled script usage. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Local Clinical File Processing Uses User-Selected Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several helper scripts read clinical documents and optionally write reports, tables, or copied templates to paths supplied by the user. This is expected for the skill, but it can expose protected health information or overwrite files if used carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed scripts directly open input files and write outputs when an output argument is supplied. The behavior is local and legitimate, but the clinical data context makes mishandling more sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Enabled Workflow Runs Local Helper Scripts
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with limited impact: The skill declares Bash access and documentation recommends running local Python utilities. No unsafe shell construction was found, but users should review commands before execution because clinical files may contain sensitive data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly enables Bash and shows Python script commands. I did not find subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec usage, so the concern is operational rather than code injection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Medical Text False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-volume weak cryptographic algorithm alerts occur in clinical prose, abbreviations, examples, and validation pattern names. No cryptographic implementation or insecure cipher use was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines contain clinical descriptions, file names, or table examples rather than cryptographic code. The Python scripts reviewed import standard parsing modules, not crypto libraries.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static analyzer treated markdown inline code and fenced examples as Ruby shell backticks. The reviewed files are documentation or templates, and no Ruby execution or dynamic shell interpolation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown code fences or command examples. There is no Ruby source file or backtick operator executing user-controlled content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs point to public clinical standards and professional resources. No script sends data to these URLs, and no network client code was found in the reviewed scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as documentation references. A targeted search found no requests, urllib, socket, or similar network access in the scripts.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for prompt injection concerns: Targeted review found no override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip analysis. The skill gives domain workflow guidance rather than attempting to control the evaluator.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted searches for common injection phrases returned no matches. The reviewed opening sections describe clinical-report functionality without evaluator-directed instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Output File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts accept output paths from command-line arguments and write generated reports or copied templates locally. This is useful but should be treated carefully in directories containing sensitive clinical records.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code paths are explicit and local. The risk is accidental data exposure or overwrite, not confirmed malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Draft Clinical Reports With Compliance Checks
content.value_statement: Clinical teams need consistent report structures that respect regulatory and privacy expectations. This skill provides templates, review checklists, and local validation utilities for clinical documentation workflows.
actual_capabilities: Drafts structured case reports using CARE guideline sections and consent reminders.
Provides templates for SOAP notes, history and physicals, discharge summaries, consult notes, and diagnostic reports.
Supports clinical trial documents such as SAE reports and ICH-E3 clinical study report outlines.
Checks reports for common HIPAA identifiers and age de-identification issues using local scripts.
Formats adverse event summaries and extracts basic vitals, demographics, and medication patterns from text.
Reviews terminology for prohibited abbreviations and common clinical coding patterns.
limitations: It does not replace clinician judgment, regulatory counsel, or institutional review processes.
The validation scripts use pattern matching and can miss context-dependent privacy or clinical issues.
Users must avoid submitting identifiable patient data to services that are not approved for PHI.
Some references and templates may need updates for local policy, journal rules, or current regulations.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Case Report Draft
use_cases.description: Create a CARE-aligned outline, identify missing sections, and prepare a de-identification review checklist.

use_cases.title: Standardize Patient Documentation
use_cases.description: Draft SOAP notes, history and physicals, consult notes, or discharge summaries with consistent headings.

use_cases.title: Review Trial Safety Documents
use_cases.description: Structure SAE narratives, adverse event tables, and CSR sections for clinical trial reporting.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical operations or medical writing team

prompt_templates.title: Start a SOAP Note
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a structured clinical note.
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a SOAP note from these de-identified encounter details. Keep assessment and plan concise, and flag missing clinical information.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CARE Case Report Outline
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for journal-style case report planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CARE guideline case report outline for this de-identified case. Include required sections, consent reminders, and a publication readiness checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Check Clinical Trial Reporting Completeness
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a clinical study report or safety document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this de-identified clinical trial report against ICH-E3 and SAE reporting expectations. List missing sections, unclear safety details, and suggested revisions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Privacy and Terminology Risks
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced quality review before sharing or submission.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this de-identified clinical report for HIPAA identifier risks, prohibited abbreviations, ambiguous terminology, and documentation gaps. Provide prioritized fixes.

output_examples.input: De-identified notes for a patient case report with presentation, diagnostic workup, treatment, and outcome.
output: CARE-aligned section outline with title, abstract, timeline, diagnostic assessment, intervention, outcome, discussion, and consent items.
A short list of missing details, such as patient perspective, follow-up duration, or consent documentation.
A privacy review reminder for dates, locations, identifiers, and age reporting.

output_examples.input: Adverse event records grouped by treatment arm for a clinical trial report.
output: A readable adverse event summary table grouped by treatment arm.
Counts and percentages for any adverse event, related events, serious events, deaths, and discontinuations.
Notes about columns that need source verification before regulatory use.

output_examples.input: A draft discharge summary with hospital course, medications, follow-up, and pending results.
output: A cleaner discharge summary structure with admission reason, diagnoses, hospital course, discharge condition, and follow-up tasks.
A list of missing handoff items and possible terminology issues.
A reminder to complete clinician review before signing the record.

best_practices: Use de-identified inputs unless your environment is approved for protected health information.
Run privacy and terminology checks before sharing drafts outside the care or study team.
Verify every diagnosis, medication, dose, date, and regulatory statement against source records.
anti_patterns: Do not use generated text as final clinical documentation without qualified human review.
Do not include patient identifiers in prompts, filenames, examples, or exported validation reports.
Do not treat pattern-based validation as proof of HIPAA, FDA, ICH-GCP, or journal compliance.

faq.question: Can this skill write final clinical records?
faq.answer: It can draft and structure documentation, but a qualified clinician must review and approve final records.

faq.question: Does it support case reports for journals?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes CARE-oriented guidance, templates, consent reminders, and quality checks for case reports.

faq.question: Can it process protected health information?
faq.answer: Only use PHI in environments approved by your organization. Prefer de-identified text whenever possible.

faq.question: What report types are included?
faq.answer: It covers case reports, diagnostic reports, SOAP notes, history and physicals, discharge summaries, SAE reports, and CSR outlines.

faq.question: Are the validation scripts comprehensive?
faq.answer: No. They use rule-based checks and should support, not replace, expert review and local compliance procedures.

faq.question: Does it access the internet?
faq.answer: The reviewed scripts do not make network requests. Documentation includes public links to clinical standards.
